hypothesis: Removing unproven value paths and logit softcap gives a clean MoE backbone for rebuilding
expected_result: Stable finite BPB, learned QK gamma moves, and router remains non-collapsed enough to use as the new phase baseline
interpretation: The stripped model is stable and fast but much worse than the old feature-rich stack; this is now the phase baseline, and AdamW peak 0.01 looks too aggressive or poorly matched to the stripped setup
next_hypothesis: Sweep AdamW peak LR on the {1,3}*10^n grid while keeping architecture fixed
description: remove value embeddings/value residuals and logit softcap; use fan-in truncated init, learned QK gamma, shared AdamW schedule, and shape-aware Muon LR tied to AdamW

hypothesis: AdamW peak 0.01 is too aggressive for the stripped backbone and fan-in init
expected_result: Lower matched-step train loss, cleaner router load, and better BPB without throughput or memory cost
interpretation: The lower LR improved BPB by 0.009207, reduced train CE to 2.591596, and improved load diagnostics; matched-step behavior also clearly favored 0.003 over 0.01
next_hypothesis: Test the next lower log-grid LR point 0.001 before adding architecture back
description: change only ADAMW_LR from 0.01 to 0.003, with Muon peaks still shape-aware and tied to AdamW

hypothesis: AdamW peak 0.003 may still be too sharp, and cleaner routing at 0.001 might improve BPB
expected_result: Some CE slowdown but possible validation improvement from much cleaner routing
interpretation: The router became very clean, but train CE stayed far too high and BPB collapsed; in this range learning progress matters more than additional average load uniformity
next_hypothesis: Restore 0.003 as the LR baseline and start adding architectural components back
description: change only ADAMW_LR from 0.003 to 0.001, with Muon peaks still shape-aware and tied to AdamW

hypothesis: Scalar first-value residual might restore useful attention value memory
expected_result: Alpha should move from zero and matched-step CE/router diagnostics should improve
interpretation: Aborted before validation; gradient diagnostics showed alpha grew large but first-value residual gradient stayed tiny while router load concentration worsened
next_hypothesis: Do not retry uncapped scalar value residual; only revisit with router-protected/capped/detached variants
description: add zero-init learned scalar first-value residual to later attention layers

hypothesis: Finer experts at fixed active and total expert width may improve expert-combination richness
expected_result: Matched active/total params with better BPB or at least better matched-step CE despite possible speed cost
interpretation: Aborted at step 727; matched-step loss was about 0.049 worse than baseline and throughput was lower despite cleaner load diagnostics
next_hypothesis: Restore 16/top-2/1792; do not go finer now
description: test 32 experts top-4 hidden-896 with active and total expert width fixed

hypothesis: Sigmoid affinities may reduce unhelpful global softmax competition while preserving hard top-k routes
expected_result: Equal or lower BPB with healthy load and no sigmoid saturation
interpretation: Final BPB was effectively tied but slightly worse; early CE improved, average load was cleaner, z-loss and low-affinity saturation rose, and final train CE worsened
next_hypothesis: Test loss-free expert bias once; if it fails, revert to softmax router
description: change router output weights and load-balance score mass from softmax to normalized selected sigmoid affinities

hypothesis: A fixed damped first-value mix may recover useful attention value memory without learned scalar instability
expected_result: Better BPB than simple baseline with healthier routing than learned value mix
interpretation: Fixed 0.5/0.5 improved BPB substantially and kept routing much cleaner than learned value mix; this proves the useful mechanism is not just learned scalar adaptation
next_hypothesis: Try first-heavy fixed ratio at same L1 scale
description: fixed value mix v=0.5*v1+0.5*vl on layers 1-7

hypothesis: A first-heavy fixed mix may use early value memory more effectively than equal mixing
expected_result: Lower BPB than fixed 0.5/0.5, accepting moderate load concentration only if BPB improves
interpretation: Fixed 0.75/0.25 became the best value-only result; load concentration increased, but BPB improved clearly; larger raw scales and learned variants did not beat it
next_hypothesis: Resume sigmoid expert-bias routing on this value-mix baseline
description: fixed value mix v=0.75*v1+0.25*vl on layers 1-7

hypothesis: Sigmoid affinities plus non-gradient expert bias may reduce harmful load-balance gradients while preserving hard-load control
expected_result: Lower BPB than fixed value mix with no collapse and bias below clamp
interpretation: BPB improved clearly, but one layer collapsed badly and router bias hit clamp; mechanism is promising but zero load-balance pressure is insufficient
next_hypothesis: Add a single small load-balance repair at 0.003
description: sigmoid affinities, expert bias for top-k only, no load-balance loss, fixed 0.75/0.25 value mix

hypothesis: Small load-balance pressure should repair zero-loss sigmoid-bias collapse without losing its BPB gain
expected_result: Equal or better BPB than zero-load-loss sigmoid bias with much healthier max-layer load
interpretation: The repair improved BPB and fixed load collapse; max-layer max load fell to 0.0746 and bias stayed far below clamp
next_hypothesis: Run softmax-router low-LB control to isolate mechanism
description: sigmoid affinities, expert bias for top-k only, load-balance loss 0.003, fixed 0.75/0.25 value mix

hypothesis: The sigmoid-bias gain might be mostly from lowering load-balance pressure to 0.003
expected_result: Softmax router with the same 0.003 load loss should approach sigmoid-bias BPB if coefficient change is the key mechanism
interpretation: Softmax low-LB collapsed into much worse load and worse BPB; the sigmoid-bias controller is doing real work
next_hypothesis: Keep sigmoid-bias; test whether it stabilizes normalized learned value mix
description: softmax router, load-balance loss 0.003, fixed 0.75/0.25 value mix

hypothesis: Sigmoid-bias may stabilize normalized learned value mix and allow learned ratio/scale to compound with router gains
expected_result: Healthy load plus BPB at or below the fixed-mix sigmoid-bias baseline
interpretation: Sigmoid-bias did stabilize normalized learned mix, reducing max-layer max load from 0.322 to 0.085, but BPB regressed far behind fixed 0.75/0.25 sigmoid-bias
next_hypothesis: Restore fixed 0.75/0.25 sigmoid-bias baseline; search elsewhere for next gain
description: normalized learned value mix with gamma, sigmoid affinities, expert bias, load-balance loss 0.003

hypothesis: Exclusive self attention may improve context modeling by removing the self-value direction from attention outputs while residual/FFN preserve pointwise information
expected_result: Lower matched-step CE or validation BPB with no severe router damage
interpretation: XSA improved BPB by 0.002330 over the prior best; matched-step loss was better at step 600, and router health remained acceptable though worst-layer load CV rose to 0.351
next_hypothesis: Use XSA as baseline; try query-dependent headwise gated attention initialized near identity
description: exclusive self attention on current fixed 0.75/0.25 value mix plus sigmoid-bias plus load-balance 0.003 stack

hypothesis: Headwise post-SDPA sigmoid gate may compound with XSA by modulating contextual attention per query/head
expected_result: Slight matched-step and BPB gain with no router damage
interpretation: BPB improved by 0.000166 over XSA; matched-step loss was ahead from step 360 onward and final router load was much cleaner, though the margin is small
next_hypothesis: Try gate init 0.98 to test whether less global attenuation improves the gain
description: headwise attention gate after XSA, init sigmoid 0.95, fixed 0.75/0.25 value mix, sigmoid-bias router lb0.003

hypothesis: Headwise gate init 0.98 may preserve more attention amplitude than 0.95 while retaining query/head modulation
expected_result: Equal or better BPB than 0.95 with healthy router load
interpretation: Init 0.98 improved BPB by 0.000276 over 0.95 and 0.000442 over XSA; matched-step loss was roughly tied, and final gate bias stayed closer to identity at sigmoid 0.928
next_hypothesis: Try init 0.99 once; if it regresses, keep 0.98 and move on
description: headwise attention gate after XSA, init sigmoid 0.98, fixed 0.75/0.25 value mix, sigmoid-bias router lb0.003

hypothesis: Headwise gate init 0.99 may preserve even more attention amplitude than 0.98
expected_result: Equal or better BPB than 0.98 with healthy router load
interpretation: Init 0.99 tied 0.98 but was fractionally worse by 0.000002 BPB; router health was very clean, but there is no reason to replace the 0.98 baseline
next_hypothesis: Try centered 2*sigmoid gate initialized at exact identity
description: centered comparison not run here; this row is standard sigmoid headwise gate init 0.99

hypothesis: Centered 2*sigmoid headwise gate initialized at exact identity may preserve modulation without large gate bias
expected_result: Better matched-step CE than sigmoid gate, or equal CE with cleaner gate semantics
interpretation: Aborted at step 600; early loss was much better through step 200, but by step 360 and 600 it was clearly behind the standard sigmoid gate while load was not collapsed
next_hypothesis: Restore sigmoid gate init 0.98 and move to dense early-layer MoE structure
description: centered 2*sigmoid headwise attention gate after XSA, init effective gate 1.0

hypothesis: Dense first FFN layer may remove noisy early sparse routing while preserving upper-layer MoE specialization
expected_result: Better BPB or similar BPB with faster throughput and cleaner routing
interpretation: Dense first layer was behind early at matched steps, caught up late, improved BPB by 0.001566, raised MFU to 23.30, cut total params to 496M, and kept router load very clean
next_hypothesis: Try first two dense layers at matched active width
description: first layer dense SwiGLU hidden 3584, upper 7 layers MoE, XSA, headwise gate init 0.98, sigmoid-bias router lb0.003

hypothesis: The first dense layer result suggests early sparse routing is costly, so making the first two FFN layers dense may further reduce early routing noise and improve throughput while leaving six sparse layers for specialization
expected_result: Match or beat one-dense BPB, or remain close with higher throughput and lower total params
interpretation: Two dense early layers improved fixed-wall BPB by 0.000331, reached 2778 steps and 728.2M tokens in 450s, cut total params to 438M, and kept routing very clean with mean load CV 0.0719 and max-layer max load 0.0737
next_hypothesis: Try first three dense layers to find where dense-stem benefit saturates
description: first two layers dense SwiGLU hidden 3584, upper 6 layers MoE, XSA, headwise gate init 0.98, sigmoid-bias router lb0.003

hypothesis: The two-dense wall-time win may come mostly from getting 2778 updates while the no-dense attention-gate baseline got 2515 updates
expected_result: At 2515 matched steps, two dense should fall back toward the no-dense 0.940076 BPB if the gain is only extra updates
interpretation: Two dense still beat the no-dense baseline at matched steps by 0.000881 BPB, so the dense stem has real per-step value; the larger fixed-wall gain still partly comes from faster throughput and more updates
next_hypothesis: Keep two dense as baseline, stop dense-depth tuning, and use matched-step checks whenever speed changes
description: first two layers dense SwiGLU hidden 3584 stopped at AR_MAX_STEPS=2515 with the same 2390-step LR schedule
